doc_id: Effect of Different Distance Measures on the Performance of K-Means Algorithm An Experimental Study in Matlab [arXiv 1405.7471v1]
filename: Effect of Different Distance Measures on the Performance of K-Means Algorithm An Experimental Study in Matlab [arXiv 1405.7471v1].pdf
heading: Effect of Different Distance Measures on the Performance of K-Means Algorithm: An Experimental Study in Matlab
text:
Dibya Jyoti Bora, Dr. Anil Kumar Gupta
Department Of Computer Science & Applications, Barkatullah University, Bhopal, India
Abstract:  K-means  algorithm  is  a  very  popular  clustering algorithm which is famous for its simplicity. Distance measure plays  a  very  important  rule  on  the  performance  of  this algorithm.  We  have  different  distance  measure  techniques available. But choosing a proper  technique for distance calculation is totally dependent on the type of the data that we are  going  to  cluster.  In  this  paper  an  experimental  study  is done  in  Matlab  to  cluster  the  iris  and  wine  data  sets  with different distance measures and thereby observing the variation of the performances shown.

doc_id: Effect of Different Distance Measures on the Performance of K-Means Algorithm An Experimental Study in Matlab [arXiv 1405.7471v1]
filename: Effect of Different Distance Measures on the Performance of K-Means Algorithm An Experimental Study in Matlab [arXiv 1405.7471v1].pdf
heading: 1. INTRODUCTION:
text:
Clustering  is  an  unsupervised  study.  The  main  aim  of clustering is to divide a dataset into some different subsets (known as 'clusters') such that data into a particular subset sharing  similar  properties  while  data  in  different  subset showing  different  properties  from  data  in  another  subset. Means  to say that clustering should satisfy the two properties  [1][2]:  1.  High  Cohesive  Property  and  2.  Low Coupling Property. Clustering algorithms are mainly divided into two types based on developed cluster properties:  hierarchical  and  partitional.  The  hierarchical methods,  in  general  try  to  decompose  the  dataset  of  n objects  into  a  hierarchy  of  groups  [1].  This  hierarchical decomposition  can  be  represented by a tree structure diagram  called  as  a  dendrogram  [3];  whose  root  node represents the whole dataset and each leaf node is a single object of the dataset. The clustering results can be obtained by cutting the dendrogram at different level. There are two general approaches for the hierarchical method: agglomerative and divisive [2][3][4]. Agglomerative approach  is  a  bottom  up  approach  starting  with  n-leaf nodes, letting them as individual clusters, moving upwards towards the root with some merging criteria. While divisive hierarchical  clustering  technique  is  a  top  down  approach, starting from the root node gradually splitting the data into different  clusters  downwards      based  on  the  properties  of the data.
While  in  case  of  partitional  clustering,  k  partitions  of  the datasets  with  n  objects  are  created,  where  each  partition represents a cluster, where k<= n. It tries to divide the data into subsets or partitions based on some evaluation criteria. K-Means is a very popular partitional clustering algorithm. In this paper, first of all the K-Means  algorithm is discussed and then different distance measurement techniques for K-means algorithm are mentioned. After that we  go  through  experiments  for  implementing  K-Means with different distance measurement techniques in Matlab. Based on the results of the experiments, we finally come to a conclusion about the performance of K-Means algorithm with respect to different distance measurement techniques.

heading: 2. K -MEANS ALGORITHM:
text:
The K-Means [5] is one of the famous partition clustering algorithm  [  6][7][8].  It  takes  the  input  parameter k ,  the number of clusters, and partitions a set of n objects into k clusters so that the resulting intra-cluster similarity is high but  the  inter-cluster  similarity  is  low.  The  main  idea  is  to define  k  centroids,  one  for  each  cluster.  These  centroids should  be  placed  in  a  cunning  way  because  of  different location causes different results. So, the better choice is to place them as much as possible far away from each other. The next step is to take each point belonging to a given data set and associate it to the nearest centroid. When no point is pending, the first step is completed and an early groupage is  done.  At  this  point  we  need  to  re-calculate  k  new centroids.  After  we  have  these  k  new  centroids,  a  new binding  has  to  be  done  between  the  same  data  set  points and the nearest new centroid. A loop has been generated. As a result of this loop we may notice that the k centroids change their location step by step until no more changes are done.  In  other  words  centroids  do  not  move  any  more. Finally,  this  algorithm  aims  at  minimizing  an objective function , in this case a squared error function. The objective function
,
The Formal Algorithm [8] is :
Select K points as initial centroids.
Repeat.
Form k clusters by assigning all points to the closest centroid.
Recompute the centroid of each cluster.Until the centroids do not change

figures.description:
Here's an analysis of the image based on its visual content:

**Image Description:**

The image is a flowchart depicting an algorithm, likely related to clustering. It's presented in a diagrammatic style with rectangular boxes representing steps and arrows indicating flow direction. The color scheme consists primarily of light yellow backgrounds with green and red text/symbols.

**Detailed Breakdown of Elements:**

1.  **Start:** A rounded rectangle at the top left labeled "Start" with a red circle symbol.
2.  **Number of Cluster K:** A rectangular box labeled "Number of cluster K".
3.  **Centroid:** A rectangular box labeled "Centroid".
4.  **Distance Objects to Centroids:** A rectangular box labeled "Distance objects to centroids".
5.  **Grouping based on minimum distance:** A rectangular box labeled "Grouping based on minimum distance".
6.  **No object move group?:** A diamond-shaped decision symbol labeled "No object move group?" with a plus sign (+) at the right and a minus sign (-) at the left.
7.  **End:** A rounded rectangle at the bottom right labeled "end" with a red circle symbol.

**Flow of the Algorithm (as depicted):**

The flowchart begins with the "Start" step. It proceeds to determine the number of clusters, K. Next, it calculates centroids. Then, it measures the distance between objects and their respective centroids.  Finally, it groups objects based on a minimum distance criterion. The algorithm continues until no object move group is detected (indicated by the decision diamond). At that point, the process ends.

**Text & Symbols:**

*   The text within the boxes describes the steps of the algorithm.
*   The arrows indicate the flow of execution.
*   Decision symbols are used to represent conditional branching.
*   Red circles denote termination points.

Let me know if you would like a more specific analysis or have further questions about this flowchart!

heading: 3. DISTANCE MEASUREMENTS IN K-MEANS ALGORITHMS:
text: In  K-Means  algorithm,  we  calculate  the  distance  between each  point  of  the  dataset  to  every  centroid  initialized. Based  on  the  values  found,  points  are  assigned  to  the centroid with minimum  distance.  Hence,  this  distance calculation plays the vital role in the clustering algorithm. As we know, distance between two points can be computed with different techniques available, so, our main aim is to pick up a proper technique from the available ones. But, in choosing  such  techniques,  some  important  points  to  be noted such as: the property of the data and the dimension of the dataset. In this experiment, we take 'Cityblock','Euclidean','Cosine'  and  'Correlation'-these distance  measurement techniques for distance calculations in the K-Means algorithm. Description about each technique is mentioned below:

heading: 3.1 City Block (Manhattan):
text:
The city block distance [10][11] two point a and b with k dimensions is defined as:
The name City block distance (also referred to as Manhattan  distance )  [11]  is  explained  if  we  consider two points in the xy-plane. The shortest distance between the two points is along the hypotenuse, which is the Euclidean  distance .  The City  block  distance is  instead calculated as the distance in x plus the distance in y, which is  similar  to  the  way  we  move  in  a  city  (like  Manhattan) where  we  have  to  move  around  the  buildings  instead  of going straight through.

heading: 3.2 Euclidean distance:
text:
The Euclidean distance between two points, a and b , with k dimensions is calculated as[12][13]:
Euclidean  (and  squared  Euclidean)  distances  are  usually computed from raw data, and not from standardized data. One advantage of this method is that the distance between any  two  objects  is  not  affected  by  the  addition  of  new objects to the analysis, which may  be outliers [13]. However, the distances can be greatly affected by differences in scale among the dimensions from which the distances are computed. For example, if one of the dimensions denotes a measured length in centimeters, and you  then  convert  it  to  millimeters  (by  multiplying  the values  by  10),  the  resulting  Euclidean  can  be  greatly affected  (i.e.,  biased  by  those  dimensions  which  have  a larger scale), and consequently, the results of cluster analyses  may  be  very  different.  Generally,  it  is  good practice  to  transform  the  dimensions  so  that  they  have similar scales [13].

heading: 4. EXPERIMENTS:
text:
For our experiments, we have chosen two different datasets: iris and wine datasets. Iris dataset The Iris flower data set or Fisher's Iris data set (some times also known as Anderson's Iris data) is a multivariate data set introduced by Sir Ronald Fisher (1936) as an example of discriminant analysis.  The data set consists of 50 samples from each of three species of Iris (Iris setosa, Iris virginica and Iris versicolor). Four features were measured from each sample: the length and the width of the sepals and petals, in centimeters[16]. Table(1) gives details of the iris dataset[16]:
The wine dataset [17] is the result of a chemical analysis of wines grown in the same region in Italy but derived from three different cultivars. The analysis determined the quantities  of  13  constituents  found  in  each  of  the  three types of wines. Table (2) gives details of the wine dataset [17]:
(D) Correlation Distance:
We have 'kmeans' function to perform K-means clustering in  Matlab  [18].  The  function kmeans performs  K-Means clustering, using an iterative algorithm that assigns objects to clusters so that the sum of distances from each object to its  cluster  centroid,  over  all  clusters,  is  a  minimum.  k means returns an n-by-1 vector IDX containing the cluster indices of each point. When X is a vector, kmeans treats it as an n -by-1 data matrix, regardless of its orientation.

figures.description:
Here's an analysis of the image based solely on its visual content:

**Image Description:**

The image is a line graph presented in a grayscale format. The x-axis is labeled "Silhouette Value" ranging from approximately 0 to 1. The y-axis is labeled "Cluster" and ranges from 0 to 4.  There are four distinct, solid blue bars representing clusters.

**Data Points & Trends:**

*   The first bar begins at the origin (0,0) on the x and y axes. It extends upwards to a value of approximately 0.2 on the x-axis and 1 on the y-axis.
*   The second bar starts at the origin (0,0) on the x-axis and extends upwards to a value of approximately 0.6 on the x-axis and 2 on the y-axis.
*   The third bar begins at the origin (0,0) on the x-axis and extends upwards to a value of approximately 0.8 on the x-axis and 3 on the y-axis.
*   The fourth bar starts at the origin (0,0) on the x-axis and extends upwards to a value of approximately 0.95 on the x-axis and 4 on the y-axis.

**Overall Pattern:**

All four bars are positioned along the left side of the graph, indicating that all data points fall within the first cluster. The bars increase in height as the Silhouette Value increases, suggesting a positive correlation between these two variables.

figures.caption: Figure (2.2) Cluster figure for Euclidean distance
figures.description:
Here's an analysis of the image based on its visual content:

**Image Description:**

The image is a histogram displaying Silhouette scores for three clusters resulting from a clustering algorithm (likely K-means). The x-axis represents Silhouette values ranging from approximately -0.6 to 1.0. The y-axis represents the number of data points assigned to each cluster, labeled as Cluster 1, Cluster 2, and Cluster 3.

**Data Points & Trends:**

*   **Cluster 1:**  Approximately 85% of the data points have a Silhouette value between 0.6 and 1.0. The remaining 15% have values ranging from -0.6 to approximately 0.2.
*   **Cluster 2:** Approximately 60% of the data points have a Silhouette value between 0.6 and 1.0.  The remaining 40% have values ranging from -0.6 to approximately 0.2.
*   **Cluster 3:** Approximately 20% of the data points have a Silhouette value between 0.6 and 1.0. The remaining 80% have values ranging from -0.6 to approximately 0.2.

**Overall Pattern:**

The histogram shows that Cluster 1 exhibits the highest degree of cohesion, with nearly all data points falling within the optimal Silhouette range (0.6-1.0).  Cluster 2 has a moderate level of cohesion, while Cluster 3 demonstrates the lowest cohesion, with the majority of its data points having low Silhouette values indicating poor separation from other clusters.

**Additional Notes:**

*   The horizontal line at 0.6 represents the threshold value often used to determine if a cluster is well-defined.  Clusters with most points above this line are considered good.
*   The image is presented in black and white, with blue representing the data density within each cluster.

figures.caption: Figure (2.3) Cluster figure for Cosine distance

**Image Description:**

The image is a line graph displayed in a dark blue color. The x-axis represents "Silhouette Value," ranging from approximately 0 to 1. The y-axis represents "Cluster," with three distinct clusters labeled as 1, 2, and 3. Each cluster is represented by a solid bar extending along the x-axis.

**Data Points & Trends:**

*   **Cluster 1:** The bar for Cluster 1 extends from approximately 0.18 to 0.95 on the Silhouette Value axis.
*   **Cluster 2:** The bar for Cluster 2 extends from approximately 0.30 to 0.75 on the Silhouette Value axis.
*   **Cluster 3:** The bar for Cluster 3 extends from approximately 0.51 to 1.00 on the Silhouette Value axis.

**Overall Pattern:**

The graph illustrates a silhouette analysis result, likely generated by a clustering algorithm (such as k-means).  The Silhouette Values indicate the quality of each cluster. A higher Silhouette Value generally suggests a better-defined and more cohesive cluster. Cluster 3 exhibits the highest Silhouette Value, indicating it is the most tightly clustered group. Cluster 1 has a lower value than Cluster 2, and Cluster 2 has the lowest value among the three clusters.

tables.summary:
Here's a summary of the table's structure and the information it represents:

**Structure:**

The table is organized into rows, with each row representing a different iteration (or step) in a process.  It has four columns:

*   **iter:** This column indicates the number of iterations performed.
*   **phase:** This column specifies the phase or stage within that iteration. In this case, all iterations are in "Phase 1".
*   **num:** This column shows the number of elements involved in each iteration.
*   **sum:** This column represents a cumulative sum related to the data processed during that iteration.

**Key Information Represented:**

The table likely records results from an optimization or calculation process, probably involving minimizing a "sum of distances."  Here's what we can infer:

*   **Iterations:** The table shows the progression of steps taken in the process (iterations 1 through 7).
*   **Data Volume:** The 'num' column indicates how much data is being considered at each step.
*   **Cumulative Sum:** The 'sum' column likely represents a running total or accumulated value calculated during each iteration, probably related to the distances being minimized.
*   **Best Result:**  The final row includes "Best total sum of distances = 7897.88," indicating that this particular combination of iterations (iter 1-6) resulted in the lowest overall sum of distances achieved by the process.

**In essence, it's a record of an optimization algorithm's progress, showing how different iteration numbers and data volumes contribute to finding the best solution.**

Here's a detailed analysis of the image provided, focusing solely on the visual elements:

**Image Description:**

The image is a bar chart representing Silhouette Analysis results. The x-axis is labeled "Silhouette Value," ranging from approximately -1 to +1. The y-axis is labeled "Cluster" and displays integer values from 1 to 3.  Each cluster (1, 2, and 3) has a corresponding bar.

**Data Points & Patterns:**

*   **Cluster 1:** The bar for Cluster 1 extends from approximately -0.8 to 0.9 on the Silhouette Value axis.
*   **Cluster 2:** The bar for Cluster 2 extends from approximately -0.8 to 0.9 on the Silhouette Value axis.
*   **Cluster 3:** The bar for Cluster 3 extends from approximately -0.8 to 0.2 on the Silhouette Value axis.

All three bars are of equal height, indicating that all clusters have the same silhouette score.  The x-axis shows a range of silhouette values from negative to positive.

figures.description:
Here's an analysis of the image based solely on its visual content:

**Image Description:**

The image is a bar chart presented in a scientific document. It displays data related to clustering analysis, likely performed using a method like k-means or hierarchical clustering.

**Axes and Data Points:**

*   **X-axis:** The x-axis represents the "Silhouette Value."  The scale ranges from 0 to 1.
*   **Y-axis:** The y-axis represents "Cluster". It has two distinct levels, labeled '1' and '2'.
*   **Bars:** There are five bars. Each bar corresponds to a cluster.
    *   Bar for Cluster 1:  Extends from 0 to approximately 1.
    *   Bar for Cluster 2: Extends from 0 to approximately 1.
    *   Bar for Cluster 3: Extends from 0 to approximately 1.
    *   Bar for Cluster 4: Extends from 0 to approximately 1.
    *   Bar for Cluster 5: Extends from 0 to approximately 1.

**Overall Trend & Pattern:**

All five bars are positioned at the maximum value of 1 on the silhouette value scale. This indicates that all clusters have a Silhouette Value of 1, suggesting high-quality clustering results according to the Silhouette metric. The Silhouette Value is a measure of how similar samples within a cluster are to each other compared to their similarity to samples in neighboring clusters. A value of 1 represents optimal clustering.

The image is a bar chart representing a silhouette analysis result. The x-axis is labeled "Silhouette Value" with values ranging from approximately -0.2 to 1.0. The y-axis is labeled "Cluster" with two distinct clusters indicated as Cluster 1 and Cluster 2.

**Bar Heights:**

*   **Cluster 1:**  The bar for Cluster 1 has a height of approximately 0.8.

Each cluster is represented by multiple data points, each with a silhouette value.  The specific values are not discernible from the image resolution but appear to be clustered around the following:

*   **Cluster 1:** All data points have silhouette values between approximately 0.4 and 0.8.
*   **Cluster 2:** All data points have silhouette values between approximately 0.3 and 0.6.

**Overall Trend/Pattern:**

The chart visually represents the quality of clustering achieved by a clustering algorithm. The silhouette values indicate how well each data point fits within its assigned cluster compared to other clusters.  Higher silhouette values generally suggest better-defined clusters. The chart shows that Cluster 1 has higher and more consistent silhouette values than Cluster 2, suggesting that Cluster 1 is a more cohesive and well-separated cluster.

tables.summary:
Here's a summary of the table's structure and the information it represents:

**Structure:**

The table is organized into three columns labeled "City," "Euclidean," "Cosine," and "Correlation." It has one row with data for a single city, identified as "0.069". 

**Key Information:**

*   **City:** The table represents the similarity score between a single city (labeled as "0.069")
*   **Euclidean:** This column shows the Euclidean distance between the city's data points.  Lower values indicate greater similarity.
*   **Cosine:** This column displays the cosine similarity between the city's data points. Higher values indicate greater similarity.
*   **Correlation:** This column presents the Pearson correlation coefficient, which measures the linear relationship between the city's data points. A value of 0.085 indicates a weak positive correlation.

**In essence, this table is comparing the similarity of a single city to itself based on three different distance/similarity metrics.**  The values suggest that the city has a moderate level of similarity to itself according to all three measures.

Here's a summary of the table's structure and the information it represents:

**Structure:**

The table is organized into two main columns:

*   **City:** This column lists three cities (0.078, 0.082, and 0.089).
*   **Metrics:**  This column contains three numerical values for each city - Euclidean, Cosine, and Correlation.

**Key Information:**

The table presents similarity scores between the three cities based on three different distance/similarity metrics:

*   **Euclidean Distance:** This likely represents a straight-line distance calculation (or perhaps a similar measure of difference) between the cities.
*   **Cosine Similarity:**  This measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, indicating the similarity in direction rather than magnitude. It's often used when comparing data sets with different scales.
*   **Correlation:** This indicates the degree to which the values for each city are linearly related to one another.

**In essence, the table is showing how similar these three cities are to each other according to these three different methods of measuring similarity.**  The numbers (0.078 - 0.089) represent the resulting scores from applying those metrics.

doc_id: Effect of Different Distance Measures on the Performance of K-Means Algorithm An Experimental Study in Matlab [arXiv 1405.7471v1]
filename: Effect of Different Distance Measures on the Performance of K-Means Algorithm An Experimental Study in Matlab [arXiv 1405.7471v1].pdf
heading: CONCLUSION:
text: On the  basis  of  results  of  the  experiments  done,  we  have found that city block distance shows better performance for both the datasets in terms of less computation time. While cosine takes more computation time in comparison to other distance measures for both the data sets. But, when we refer to silhouette plots of the clusters, then we must admit that 'correlation' distance measures show a better interpretation of the clustered data. We have performed our experiments on two different datasets (i.e. iris and wine) only to observe the  performance  with  respect  to  datasets  with  different number of attributes. Iris dataset has four attributes, while wine  dataset  has  thirteen  attributes.  While  choosing  a proper distance measure, we must also keep an eye on the number of attributes involved in that dataset as otherwise it will result a high time complexity if a wrong one is chosen. In  our  future  work,  we  will  consider  another  different distance measures for K-Means algorithm with respect to a big dataset and perform a comparison among  them, thereby,  try to propose a good one for the task of clustering big  data  set.  Also,  we  will  try  to  extend  our  study  for another  partition  clustering  algorithms  like  K-Medoids, CLARA and CLARANS.
